senses.definition: A system or process that sends or receives both sound and pictures together.
examples: The new audivision system allowed the team to share live sound and images with remote viewers.
Engineers tested the audivision link before the conference began.
The patent describes a limited receiving system capable of partial audivision.

senses.definition: A female first name.
examples: Audrey invited all her friends to the party.
My grandmother’s name was Audrey.
Audrey started a new job last month.

senses.definition: John James Audubon, a famous 19th‑century American naturalist and painter known for his detailed pictures of birds.
examples: Audubon spent years traveling across North America to study birds.
The museum has an original Audubon print.
Audubon’s work helped inspire later bird conservation efforts.

senses.definition: The Audubon Society, a US organization that works to protect birds and nature.
examples: She volunteers with the local Audubon chapter.
Audubon supports projects that restore bird habitats.
The Audubon Society organizes bird‑watching walks in the area.

senses.definition: Relating to or in the style of Audubon, especially in detailed observation or painting of birds.
examples: The guide praised his audubonistic sketches of the local birdlife.
Her audubonistic approach combines art and science.
The magazine featured a series of audubonistic illustrations.

senses.definition: A cry of pain, surprise, or complaint in some dialects of English or in creative writing.
examples: “Aue!” he shouted as the hot pan burned his hand.
Aue, that really hurt!
She let out a soft “aue” when she bumped her head.

senses.definition: Short form used in English texts mainly for the German preposition "auf," meaning "on" or "onto," especially in language examples.
examples: In the phrase "auf Deutsch," auf means "in" or "in the German language."
The textbook gives several examples with auf followed by the dative case.
They discussed when to use auf with movement verbs.

senses.definition: Another spelling of "au fait": fully familiar with something or well informed about it.
examples: She is not really aufait with the latest software updates.
You need to be aufait with current regulations before advising clients.
The manager expects new staff to be aufait with company policies.

senses.definition: A German word sometimes used in English texts to mean a task, duty, or exercise, especially in teaching or academic contexts.
examples: Each student received a short Aufgabe to complete at home.
The listening Aufgabe was more difficult than the reading one.
Her main Aufgabe in the project was to collect the data.

senses.definition: Plural of the German loanword "Aufgabe," meaning several tasks, duties, or exercises.
examples: The workbook includes many listening Aufgabes for practice.
He divided the Aufgabes among the members of the group.
These Aufgabes focus on basic grammar patterns.

senses.definition: A German word used in English discussions of history or philosophy to refer to the Enlightenment.
examples: The professor traced the roots of modern thought back to the Aufklärung.
Many scholars see the Aufklärung as a turning point in European history.
Her research focuses on religious debates during the Aufklärung.

senses.definition: Short form found in English texts for the German preposition "aufs" (a contraction of "auf das"), used mainly in language examples.
examples: In this phrase, aufs is a combination of auf and das.
The grammar book explains when to use aufs versus auf.
Learners often confuse aufs with similar forms in German.

senses.definition: A German word used in English mainly in music or event writing to mean an upbeat or the opening of something.
examples: The concert served as the Auftakt to the summer festival.
The short piano piece functions as an Auftakt to the longer work.
This meeting is just the Auftakt for deeper negotiations.

senses.definition: Written short form for the month of August.
examples: The report is due on 5 Aug.
Their last meeting was in Aug 2025.
The store will reopen in early Aug.

senses.definition: A rare mineral; a type of silicate found in certain rocks.
examples: The geologist identified small crystals of auganite in the sample.
Auganite occurs together with several other silicate minerals.
The presence of auganite suggests a particular geological history.

senses.definition: Extremely dirty, difficult, or needing a lot of effort to clean up or improve, especially in a moral or political sense.
examples: The new minister faced the Augean task of cleaning up widespread corruption.
Reforming the justice system turned out to be an Augean labor.
The committee began the Augean job of reviewing decades of mismanagement.

senses.definition: A mineral made mainly of aluminum phosphate, usually found in small green or white crystals.
examples: The museum displays a rare piece of augelite.
Augelite often occurs in quartz veins.
They analyzed the augelite crystals under the microscope.

senses.definition: In geology, large eye‑shaped mineral grains in a type of rock called gneiss.
examples: The rock shows clear feldspar augen scattered through the matrix.
Under the hand lens, several augen become visible.
Augen in the gneiss indicate strong deformation of the rock.

senses.definition: In mathematics, the number to which another number is added.
examples: In the sum 5 + 3, 5 is the augend and 3 is the addend.
Teachers often explain which number is the augend in simple addition.
The augend remains the same while different addends are tested.

senses.definition: Plural of augend: several numbers that other numbers are added to.
examples: The students practiced with different augends and addends.
These augends can be combined with many values.
The table lists possible augends in the first column.

senses.definition: A tool with a helical metal part used for drilling holes in wood, earth, or ice.
examples: He used an auger to drill holes in the fence posts.
The workers lowered the auger into the ground to take soil samples.
An ice auger is essential for safe ice fishing.

senses.definition: To drill or bore a hole using an auger.
examples: They augered several test holes along the planned route.
The farmer augered into the soil to install irrigation pipes.
Technicians augered through the ice to reach the water below.

senses.definition: A person or machine that uses an auger.
examples: The augerer checked the depth of each hole.
Modern augerers can drill quickly through hard soil.
The company hired an experienced augerer for the project.

senses.definition: Plural of auger: several tools used for drilling holes in materials like wood, earth, or ice.
examples: The workshop keeps different augers for various materials.
They loaded the augers onto the truck before leaving.
Ice fishers often bring spare augers.

senses.definition: A small channel, bucket, or trough used to carry water, especially in old machinery.
examples: Water poured from each auget as the wheel turned.
The engineer replaced several damaged augets on the mill wheel.
An old diagram showed the wooden augets fixed around the rim.

senses.definition: A written representation of a sound expressing annoyance, disgust, or pain.
examples: Augh, I spilled coffee on my notes again.
“Augh!” she cried as the door slammed on her finger.
Augh, this traffic is terrible.

senses.definition: Anything at all.
examples: If you hear aught about her condition, please let me know.
He denied that he knew aught of the missing documents.
The report offered little, if aught, in the way of new evidence.

senses.definition: Zero, especially when saying a year or a number, in older usage.
examples: He was born in aught‑six, as his grandfather liked to say.
They played baseball together back in aught‑one.
The old man talked about the aughts as a simpler time.

senses.definition: At all; in any way, in old or dialect use.
examples: He cared not aughtlins for their opinion.
She was not aughtlins surprised by the news.
They seemed aughtlins interested, but they soon left.

senses.definition: The years from 2000 to 2009, especially in informal American English.
examples: He started his career in the early aughts.
Many popular blogs began in the mid‑aughts.
Fashion from the aughts is becoming trendy again.

senses.definition: A dark, common mineral found in volcanic rocks, belonging to the pyroxene group.
examples: The lava sample contained a high proportion of augite.
Under the microscope, augite crystals appear dark and blocky.
Augite is typical of many basaltic rocks.

senses.definition: Plural of augite: several grains or crystals of the mineral augite.
examples: Augites dominate the dark minerals in this rock.
The thin section revealed many augites scattered in the matrix.
These augites vary in size and shape.

senses.definition: Containing or relating to augite.
examples: The rock shows an augitic texture under the microscope.
Augitic minerals dominate the darker parts of the sample.
They described the lava as strongly augitic.

senses.definition: A type of dark volcanic rock made mainly of augite.
examples: The geologist classified the sample as an augitite.
Augitite is usually dark and fine‑grained.
They found layers of augitite above the older sediments.

senses.definition: A porphyritic rock that has large augite crystals in a finer groundmass of potash feldspar.
examples: The cliff is made mainly of augitophyre.
Under the microscope, the augitophyre clearly shows large augite crystals.
They mapped several bodies of augitophyre in the region.

senses.definition: To increase or add to something, especially by adding extra amounts.
examples: She took a part‑time job to augment her income.
The company plans to augment its staff with several new hires.
They augmented the team’s skills by bringing in an external consultant.

senses.definition: In grammar or music, something that is added to make a form or interval larger.
examples: In some languages, the augment is a prefix added to past tense forms.
The composer uses an augment of the theme in the second movement.
Students learned how the augment changes the basic verb stem.

senses.definition: That can be increased or made larger.
examples: The budget is still augmentable if more funds are approved.
They focused on augmentable aspects of the program, such as training hours.
The system’s storage is easily augmentable.

senses.definition: The act of increasing something or making it larger in amount or size.
examples: The company approved the augmentation of its research budget.
Augmentation of staff will improve customer service.
The project depends on the augmentation of existing resources.

senses.definition: Someone or something that causes an increase in size, amount, or value.
examples: The new policy acted as a strong augmentationer of local investment.
Technological advances can be powerful augmentationers of productivity.
He became the main augmentationer of the team’s confidence.

senses.definition: Plural of augmentation: several increases in size, amount, or value.
examples: Several augmentations of staff were required during the busy season.
The report details recent augmentations in the defense budget.
These augmentations have improved system performance.

senses.definition: Serving to increase the size, force, or degree of something.
examples: The therapist suggested augmentative strategies to support speech.
They adopted an augmentative approach to their training program.
The drug has an augmentative effect when combined with therapy.

senses.definition: In linguistics, describing a word form that expresses a bigger size or stronger meaning than the base word.
examples: The language uses augmentative suffixes to indicate large size.
Students compared diminutive and augmentative forms.
An augmentative noun can suggest size, emotion, or emphasis.

senses.definition: In a way that increases size, amount, or effect.
examples: The medicine works augmentatively with other treatments.
The new tools operate augmentatively rather than replacing existing ones.
The policy acts augmentatively on economic growth.

senses.definition: Plural of augmentative: word forms that show larger size or stronger meaning than the base word.
examples: The language uses several augmentatives to express big size or strong feeling.
Students listed diminutives and augmentatives in two columns.
These augmentatives can also carry emotional meanings.

senses.definition: Increased in size, amount, or effect.
examples: The team worked with an augmented budget this year.
Their augmented staff handled the extra workload well.
The data set is augmented with new entries each month.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of augment: increased or added to something.
examples: They augmented their savings by taking freelance work.
The company augmented production during the holiday season.
New evidence has augmented the case against the suspect.

senses.definition: In an increased or enlarged way.
examples: The benefits are now provided augmentedly to all employees.
Profits grew augmentedly after the merger.
Resources were distributed augmentedly in response to demand.

senses.definition: Someone or something that increases the size, amount, or effect of something.
examples: The new feature is a strong augmenter of user engagement.
Foreign investment has become an important augmenter of local jobs.
The extra training acted as an augmenter of their confidence.

senses.definition: Plural of augmenter: people or things that increase size, amount, or effect.
examples: These policies are significant augmenters of social welfare.
Technology and training are key augmenters of productivity.
The measures became strong augmenters of public trust.

senses.definition: Present participle of augment: increasing something or adding to it.
examples: She is augmenting her income by teaching online.
The company is augmenting its staff to handle new projects.
They are augmenting the data set with new surveys.

senses.definition: Another form of "augmentative": serving to increase the size, amount, or effect of something.
examples: The program offers several augmentive supports for learners.
They introduced augmentive measures to strengthen security.
The drug has an augmentive impact when combined with others.

senses.definition: Something that increases power, size, or effect, especially a device in an engine or system.
examples: The jet engine’s augmentor boosts thrust during takeoff.
They installed an augmentor to improve fuel efficiency.
A new augmentor was added to the system to handle peak loads.

senses.definition: Plural of augmentor: devices or things that increase power, size, or effect.
examples: The new aircraft is equipped with advanced augmentors.
These augmentors help the system cope with sudden demand.
Engineers tested several augmentors before choosing a final design.
